title: Quick check: agent upgrades and version compliance

choices: The agent self-upgrades. The user sees nothing;

choices.label: Manual upgrades are the default; auto-upgrade has to be enabled per agent.
choices.rationale: Self-upgrade is the default.

choices.label: The customer's super-admin approves each upgrade.
choices.rationale: The agent handles it.

choices.label: Upgrades require a reboot signed off by the user.
choices.rationale: Not part of the normal flow.

questions.explanation: The portal's version column updates. Manual upgrades are a symptom, not routine. Self-upgrade is the design; manual touch means something interrupted the normal flow.

questions.prompt: 'An upgrade failure is rarely its own diagnostic class.' What pattern does the lesson teach?

choices.label: Upgrade failures are always caused by Huntress's back end.
choices.rationale: Most upgrade failures are MSP/customer-side.

choices.label: Upgrade failures are always benign and self-resolving.
choices.rationale: Stale agents drift out of effective coverage; not benign.

choices.label: Upgrade failures only happen on macOS.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform pattern.

questions.explanation: (AV, policy, blocked outbound. Or resource shortage) showing up again on a different operation. Same diagnostic toolkit; different trigger.

questions.prompt: Why is 'online with a stale version' a problem despite the agent still phoning home?
choices: Silent drift out of effective coverage.

choices.label: Stale agents send extra telemetry that costs more.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

choices.label: Stale agents trigger Critical incidents in the SOC queue.
choices.rationale: Not how the SOC operates.

choices.label: Stale agents disable themselves after 90 days.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: The SOC's detections shift over time; an agent that can't take updates loses ground gradually. Online and stale is the silent-drift state; the version number is the only signal.

questions.prompt: Why is 'upgrading by hand without finding the cause' called out as wrong?
choices: A manual upgrade fixes one endpoint;

choices.label: Manual upgrades void the warranty.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

choices.label: Manual upgrades require senior sign-off every time.
choices.rationale: Manual upgrades are a symptom-treatment regardless of who runs them.

choices.label: Manual upgrades take longer than re-installs.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's reason.

questions.explanation: The next time the agent needs to upgrade, the same wall is in place and the endpoint goes stale again. Fix the cause, not the symptom. Manual upgrades treat a symptom and guarantee the same problem next time.

questions.prompt: Quarterly review: 18 of 198 agents at a customer are two or more versions behind the target. Other customers in your portfolio show single-digit drift. What's the first investigation?

choices.label: Manually upgrade all 18 from the portal or RMM, one at a time.
choices.rationale: If the cause is shared, they re-stale next month. Find the cause first.

choices: Look at the 18 endpoints for patterns.

choices.label: Tell the customer's IT they have a problem with their endpoints.
choices.rationale: You haven't diagnosed; the problem might be on the MSP's side.

questions.explanation: Same OU, same site, same OS patch level, same AV policy scope? Eighteen on one customer is the cluster shape that almost always has a shared cause.

questions.prompt: Pattern investigation finds: all 18 are on the customer's 'tier-2 user' GPO scope with a tighter AppLocker policy. The policy allow-listed Huntress paths months ago, but the new agent version's binary path is slightly different and isn't allowed. What do you do?
choices: Ask the customer's IT to update the AppLocker GPO allow-list with the new Huntress agent binary path, push the policy.

choices.label: Remove the 18 endpoints from the tier-2 GPO scope.
choices.rationale: Weakening the customer's GPO to fit our tooling is hostile.

choices.label: Upgrade the agents using a PowerShell bypass of AppLocker on each endpoint.
choices.rationale: Works once, next upgrade fails the same way, and you've taught the runbook a bypass.

questions.explanation: Then re-attempt the upgrades. Document in the PSA so the next agent-version bump triggers a check of the same policy. Cause identified, fix at the policy level, all 18 unstick with one change.

questions.prompt: How do you check version compliance?

choices.label: Email each endpoint's owner asking which version is installed.
choices.rationale: Out of scope; the portal exposes the data.

choices.label: Run a PowerShell script across all endpoints to query the local agent.
choices.rationale: Unnecessarily heavyweight; the portal is the canonical source.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket asking for a compliance report.
choices.rationale: Misuses support.

questions.explanation: Sort or filter by version; identify the target version; flag agents more than one or two versions behind. The Agents view exposes the data; the portal is the canonical source.

questions.prompt: The agent upgrades but the new version produces immediate offline or service-crash symptoms. What's the guidance?
choices: Rollback territory; senior and Huntress support own that path.

choices.label: Roll back yourself via PowerShell.
choices.rationale: Same anti-pattern as PowerShell bypass; not a tech-level action.

choices.label: Treat as a new install failure (lessons 14-17) and proceed.
choices.rationale: Misclassifies. A new-version regression is its own escalation surface.

choices.label: Ignore it; the next upgrade will fix it.
choices.rationale: Hides the failure during a coverage gap.

questions.prompt: After fixing an upgrade-failure cluster, what should you watch for?
choices: No spike in offline agents in the same customer immediately after the fix.

choices.label: A spike in incident volume from the upgraded agents.
choices.rationale: Not the named symptom.

choices.label: The customer's super-admin signing off via email.
choices.rationale: Not a lesson workflow.

choices.label: Huntress support confirming the new version via ticket.
choices.rationale: The portal exposes the version state.

questions.explanation: Upgrades that break the agent are rare but do happen. Verification isn't just 'version updated'; it's 'agents stayed online after the update.'

questions.prompt: When multiple endpoints in the same customer are stuck on the same older version, the right move is to look for a shared cause (OU, AV policy, network site, OS patch level) before attempting upgrades one by one.
questions.explanation: One investigation, one fix, many endpoints unstick.
